Christianity teaches us to give a reason for the hope that we have, and also to show honor to those who deserve it, without flattery or fear of people. Agrippa was very knowledgeable about the Old Testament Scriptures, so he could better judge the disagreement concerning Jesus being the Messiah. Ministers should expect to be heard patiently when they preach the faith of Christ. Paul says that he still adhered to all the good things in which he was initially educated and trained. See what his religion was like: He was a moral person, a man of virtue, and had not learned the deceptive or greedy ways of the Pharisees; he could not be accused of any open vice or irreverence. He held firm in his faith. He always deeply respected the ancient promise that God made to the fathers, and based his hope on it. The apostle knew that this would not justify him before God, but he believed it was important for his reputation among the Jews, as proof that he was not the kind of person they portrayed him to be. Although he considered these things a loss compared to gaining Christ, he mentioned them when it could honor Christ. See what Paul’s religion is like: He no longer has the same zeal for the ceremonial law that he did in his youth; the sacrifices and offerings prescribed by it are replaced by the great Sacrifice which they symbolized. He does not feel guilty about ceremonial cleansings, and believes the Levitical priesthood has been superseded by Christ's priesthood; however, regarding the main principles of his religion, he is as zealous as ever. Christ and heaven are the two central teachings of the gospel: that God has given us eternal life, and this life is in his Son. These are the matters of the promise made to the fathers. The temple service, or continuous cycle of religious duties day and night, was maintained as a declaration of faith in the promise of eternal life and in anticipation of it. The prospect of eternal life should motivate us to be diligent and steadfast in all our religious practices. Yet the Sadducees hated Paul for preaching about the resurrection; and other Jews joined them because he testified that Jesus had risen and was the promised Redeemer of Israel. Many things are considered unbelievable only because the infinite nature and perfections of Him who revealed, performed, or promised them are overlooked. Paul acknowledged that while he was a Pharisee, he was a bitter enemy to Christianity. This was his character and way of life at the beginning; and there were many obstacles preventing him from becoming a Christian. Those who have been very strict in their conduct before turning to God will later see ample reason for humbling themselves, even regarding things they once thought should have been done.

Paul became a Christian by Divine power; through a revelation of Christ both to him and within him, while he was deeply involved in sin. He was made a minister by Divine authority: the same Jesus who appeared to him in that glorious light instructed him to preach the gospel to the Gentiles. A world living in darkness must be enlightened; those who are unaware of what brings them everlasting peace must come to know it. A world consumed by wickedness must be sanctified and reformed; it is not enough for people to have their eyes opened, they must also have their hearts renewed; it is not enough to turn from darkness to light, but they must turn from the power of Satan to God. All who turn from sin to God are not only forgiven, but receive a rich inheritance. Forgiveness of sins makes this possible. No one can be happy without being holy; and to be saints in heaven we must first be saints on earth. We are made holy and saved by faith in Christ; through which we rely on Christ as our Righteousness, and surrender ourselves to him as our Ruler; through this we receive forgiveness of sins, the gift of the Holy Spirit, and eternal life. The cross of Christ was a stumbling block to the Jews, and they were angry at Paul’s preaching about the fulfillment of Old Testament prophecies. It was foretold that Christ would be the first to rise from the dead, as the Head or principal One. Also, it was prophesied that the Gentiles would come to know God through the Messiah; so why should the Jews be upset about this? Thus, a true convert can give an explanation of their hope and a good account of the change seen in them. Yet many have been blamed and persecuted for going around and urging people to repent and turn to God.

In all situations, we should speak truthfully and with careful consideration, so that we do not need to be troubled by unfair criticism from others. Those who actively and diligently follow the gospel are often looked down upon as dreamers or madmen for believing such doctrines and wonderful facts; and for attesting that the same faith and diligence, and an experience like their own, are necessary for all people, regardless of their status, in order to be saved. But apostles and prophets, and God’s Son himself, were subjected to this charge; and no one should be discouraged by it when Divine grace has made them wise unto salvation. Agrippa saw good reason for Christianity. His understanding and judgment were temporarily convinced, but his heart was not changed. His conduct and temperament were very different from the humility and spirituality of the gospel. Many are almost persuaded to become religious, but they are not quite persuaded; they have strong convictions about their duty and the excellence of God’s ways, yet they do not pursue those convictions. Paul urged that it is everyone's responsibility to become a true Christian; that there is enough grace in Christ for all. He expressed his firm belief in the truth of the gospel, and the absolute necessity of faith in Christ for salvation. The gospel of Christ offers such salvation from bondage to the Gentiles—to a lost world. Yet it is difficult for anyone to be convinced that they need a work of grace on their heart, like the one needed for the conversion of the Gentiles. Let us avoid hesitation in our own actions; and remember how far being almost persuaded to be a Christian is from being truly one.
